input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin’s computational model derives from a multi‑level Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) approach.  At the highest abstraction a SysML model captures stakeholder requirements; at the functional and logical layers SysML use‑cases and IBDs delineate component interactions.  The logical architecture is often expressed in an ADL such as ACME, AADL or MontiArc; these describe components, their interfaces, and composition, and may incorporate behavioral extensions (MontiArc Automaton or SysML v1.7 state machines) to capture dynamic behavior.  Model relationships are therefore hierarchical: a SysML functional architecture feeds into a MontiArc or ACME logical model, which is refined into a product/technical model that may be targeted by code generators for C++ or Java or simulation tools like MathWorks Simulink.  Data flows into the twin from two primary sources: real‑time sensor streams (not specified in this document) and simulation outputs or code‑generated metrics.  Data management adopts a multi‑notation strategy, storing model artifacts in a model repository, linking abstract to concrete artifacts via model‑to‑model transformations, and synchronizing state via model‑to‑data (M2D) interfaces.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Automated, continuous one‑way data stream from the physical system to the digital twin, carrying raw sensor time‑series (e.g., voltage, temperature, position) that are ingested by the twin for model‑based state estimation and analytics. The content is structured as the physical state variables captured at regular intervals and is typically transmitted via a dedicated data acquisition interface, but the specific protocol or encoding is not defined in the source.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: Digital Twin development follows a four‑stage MBSE lifecycle: (1) Requirements – expressed with SysML requirements diagrams; (2) Functional Architecture – defined with component‑and‑connector ADLs (ACME, AADL, MontiArc); (3) Logical Architecture – models component behavior with MontiArc Automata or SysML v1.7 state machines; (4) Product/Technical – translated into executable C++/Java code and validated by simulation in MathWorks Simulink. Each stage uses a distinct modeling notation tailored to its abstraction level.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Engineering of the digital twin combines specific MBSE methodologies—RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT—with component‑and‑connector ADLs (ACME, AADL, MontiArc). Functional and logical architectures are captured in these ADLs; behavioral details are modeled by MontiArc Automata or SysML v1.7 state machines. The functional models are then instantiated as executable C++ or Java code or simulated in MathWorks Simulink, providing a feedback loop for iterative refinement and validation.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The digital shadow component of the twin collects live sensor data and exposes it as one‑way data flow to a dashboard. It performs real‑time analytics that detect operational anomalies, such as joint friction, and supplies this insight to the control system, thereby improving robot control accuracy. Users interact through a visualization interface that links live data streams to the corresponding digital model, allowing operators to assess condition and apply informed decisions.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Standardization of the twin’s modeling artifacts follows well‑established model‑based engineering specifications. SysML (v1.7) is used for requirements and behavioral modeling, ACME and AADL provide an architecture description language for component interconnection, and MontiArc supplies a formal automaton language for component behavior. For simulation and executable validation, MathWorks Simulink is employed. These technologies align with the industry’s MBSE standards and facilitate interoperability between disparate modeling tools.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly describes the physical system under study: a MABI MAX 100 industrial robot. The text details its components, such as the motor, gearbox, and controllers. Quote: 'The focus of this paper is the serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes from the Swiss company MABI Robotic AG'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the text describes specific services provided by the digital shadow, such as identifying friction, improving accuracy, and visualization. Quote: 'The resulting digital shadows enable the continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction... to enhance the robot’s motion accuracy.'

The extraction states 'Not in Document', yet the source text names several specific tools and technologies used for implementation, including SysML, Simulink, MQTT, and Asset Administration Shells. Quote: 'Our methodology involves... implementing Asset Administration Shells (AAS), and live-data linking... MathWorks MATLAB Simulink'.

block_5_judge.reasoning: The extracted description lists a four-stage MBSE lifecycle (Requirements, Functional Architecture, Logical Architecture, Product/Technical) and associated tools (SysML, ACME, AADL, MontiArc, Simulink). This content is found verbatim in the Source Documents under Section II.A, specifically: 'modeling is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g., requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layer' and mentions 'SysML', 'ACME, AADL, MontiArc', and 'MathWorks Simulink'. However, the source presents this as general background information ('To better understand our approach, we describe relevant modeling methods') rather than the specific lifecycle stages executed for the Digital Shadow in the paper's use case. The actual implementation in Section IV follows a specific purpose-driven method with different steps (Problem identification, Asset analysis, etc.). The extraction is accurate to the text provided but applies general background methodology as the specific system's lifecycle without evidence that the full four-stage process was utilized for this specific twin.

The extraction describes the engineering process as combining RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT with ADLs. While these methodologies are listed in Section II.A as examples ('different methodologies on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP...'), the paper explicitly states the specific method used in this work: 'we apply this method [from 15] and have further refined it within this use case', which consists of four steps: Problem identification, Asset and model analysis, Use or creation of calculations or models, and Data identification. The extraction ignores the specific 4-step process detailed in Section IV and instead describes a generic MBSE approach mentioned only in the background. It does not accurately reflect the actual 'twinning process' used for the MABI robot described in the text.

The extraction correctly identifies the 'one-way data flow' characteristic of the digital shadow, citing the definition in Section II.B: 'an automated one-way data flow between the state of an existing physical object and a digital object'. It correctly identifies the goal of detecting 'joint friction' to 'improve robot control accuracy', supported by the Abstract ('integrate... models... to enhance our understanding of joint friction and ultimately improve robot motion accuracy') and Section IV ('data should then be applied to improve the robot control’s accuracy'). It mentions a dashboard for visualization, supported by the Abstract ('visualization in a dashboard'). However, the claim that insights are 'supplied... to the control system' as an active loop is slightly unsupported; the text states the data 'should be applied to improve accuracy' but the shadow is described as a 'passive entity' that requires 'active components to interpret' it. The extraction is mostly accurate but implies a direct control loop not fully detailed in the snippets.

The extraction claims standardization follows SysML v1.7, ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and MathWorks Simulink. While SysML is used in the specific implementation (Section IV, 'Fig. 3 shows the robot’s structure as a component & connector SysML model') and Simulink is used for simulation (Section IV), the other languages (ACME, AADL, MontiArc) are only mentioned in Section II.A as general examples of ADLs ('e.g., ACME, AADL, MontiArc'). There is no evidence in the text that these specific languages were used as standards for the twin's modeling artifacts in this specific project. The extraction extrapolates the availability of these tools from the background section into the specific solution architecture without support.
